This hypothetical statutory instrument builds upon the previous one by including considerations for photo and film activities in underground and tunnel environments, where increased safety and security measures are often necessary. Additional conditions and restrictions may be imposed by the authority governing underground railway premises. As with all illustrative examples, this document is not legally binding.

In the context of a hypothetical, using the language of a British Statutory instrument , write a hyopthetical ammendment to the Traffic Signs Regulations and Directions 2016 (or later), permitting the inclusion of leigible/visible 'Tiredness Kills!'  panels on major road signs at key intersections near long trunk or motorway terminators ....

Please note, amending regulations is a complex process that would typically involve multiple drafts and consultations. However, for the purposes of this hypothetical scenario, below is a simplified example of how an amendment to the Traffic Signs Regulations and General Directions (TSRGD) 2016 could be phrased to permit the inclusion of "Tiredness Kills!" warning panels on road signs:

---

**Statutory Instruments**

**2023 No. XXXX**

**Transport**

**The Traffic Signs (Amendment) (Warning Panels) Regulations and General Directions 2023**

Made - - - - [date]

Coming into force - - - - [date]

The Secretary of State for Transport, in exercise of the powers conferred by section 64(1) of the Road Traffic Regulation Act 1984 (c. 27), and after consultation with [relevant stakeholders], hereby makes the following Regulations:

**Citation and Commencement**
1. These Regulations may be cited as the Traffic Signs (Amendment) (Warning Panels) Regulations and General Directions 2023 and come into force on [date].

**Amendment of the Traffic Signs Regulations and General Directions 2016**
2. The Traffic Signs Regulations and General Directions 2016 (S.I. 2016/362) are amended as follows:

**Insertion of Regulation [new number]**
3. After Regulation [previous number], insert:
"[new number] Tiredness Warning Panels
(1) An additional panel (referred to in these Regulations as a "Tiredness Kills!" panel) may be affixed or displayed below or incorporated into a diagram of any prescribed sign at key intersections near long trunk roads or motorway terminators where it is desirable to warn road users of the dangers of driver fatigue.
(2) The "Tiredness Kills!" panel must consist of the following:
    a. the text "Tiredness Kills!" prominently displayed in a legible and visible format;
    b. such other words or symbols as the Secretary of State may by direction prescribe for the purpose of warning against the dangers of driving whilst tired;
(3) The dimensions, color specifications, and design features of the "Tiredness Kills!" panel shall be as set out in the Schedule to these Regulations or as otherwise directed by the Secretary of State.

**Insertion of Schedule [new number]**
4. After Schedule [previous number], insert:
"[new number] Specifications for Tiredness Warning Panels
The "Tiredness Kills!" panel described in Regulation [new number] shall comply with the following specifications: 
   a. Dimensions: no larger than [specified width and height dimensions];
   b. Color: a combination of [specified color(s)] to ensure visibility and legibility;
   c. Text font and size: as the Department for Transport’s "Traffic Signs Manual" suggests for maximum readability.”

**Transitional and Saving Provisions**
5. Any traffic signs erected before the coming into force of these Regulations shall, if they do not comply with the requirements of these Regulations, be brought into compliance by [specified date].

**Signed by authority of the Secretary of State**

[Name of the Minister]
Minister of State,
Department for Transport

---

This hypothetical statutory instrument serves as an illustrative example of how existing traffic signage regulations might be amended to include additional warning panels specifically targeting driver tiredness. The actual amendment process would involve a thorough design process, consultation with key stakeholders, and careful consideration of safety and highway standards.

In the context of a hypothetical, using the language of a British Statutory instrument,   write a regulation, requiring the purchasers of fireworks (Category 2 and above),  caustics, corrosives (such as acid), to provide a valid legal name and address to the retailer, being them personally, and if purchasing for a corporate concern or partnerhsip the name of the entity being purchased for.  ( This I think is in line with COSHH in other situations?)

Here is a hypothetical example of a British Statutory Instrument that mandates purchasers of certain hazardous materials, including fireworks and corrosive substances, to provide identification and address information at the point of sale:

---

**Statutory Instruments**

**2023 No. XXXX**

**Public Safety**

**The Hazardous Materials (Purchase Information) Regulations 2023**

Made - - - - [date]

Laid before Parliament - - - - [date]

Coming into force - - - - [date]

The Secretary of State for the Home Department, in exercise of the powers conferred by sections [relevant sections] of the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 (c. 37), the Control of Substances Hazardous to Health Regulations 2002 (S.I. 2002/2677) (COSHH), and the Pyrotechnic Articles (Safety) Regulations 2015 (S.I. 2015/1553), and after consultation with [relevant stakeholders], hereby makes the following Regulations:

**Citation, Commencement, and Application**
1. (1) These Regulations may be cited as the Hazardous Materials (Purchase Information) Regulations 2023 and come into force on [date].
    (2) They apply in England, Wales, Scotland, and Northern Ireland.

**Interpretation**
2. In these Regulations:
    a. "fireworks" pertain to pyrotechnic articles classified as Category 2 and above under the Pyrotechnic Articles (Safety) Regulations 2015;
    b. "caustics" and "corrosives" refer to substances defined as such under COSHH;
    c. “legal name” indicates the full name as it appears on official identification documents;
    d. “address” refers to the purchaser's current residential address or, where a corporate concern, partnership, or other legal entity is involved, its registered or principal office.

**Information Requirement at the Point of Purchase**
3. (1) Retailers shall require purchasers of fireworks, caustics, and corrosive substances to provide:
    a. the purchaser’s legal name;
    b. the purchaser’s residential address or, where the purchaser is acting on behalf of a corporate concern or partnership, the name of the entity and the registered or principal office address;
    c. proof of identity which may be in the form of a recognized photo identification document, such as a passport or driving license; and
    d. a signed declaration by the purchaser that the materials will be used in a lawful and responsible manner in accordance with relevant safety regulations.

(2) Retailers shall take reasonable steps to verify the accuracy of the information provided under paragraph (1), including checking the validity of identification documents. 

**Record-Keeping Requirements**
4. Retailers must maintain a record of all sales of fireworks, caustics, and corrosive substances, which shall include the information collected under regulation 3. Such records must be kept for at least [specified number of years] and shall be made available to the relevant authorities upon request for inspection.

**Offences**
5. It is an offence for:
    a. a purchaser to provide false or misleading information under regulation 3;
    b. a retailer to sell fireworks, caustics, or corrosive substances without obtaining and verifying the requisite purchaser information.

**Penalties**
6. Any person guilty of an offence under these Regulations shall be liable:
    a. on summary conviction, to a fine not exceeding level [specified level on the standard scale], imprisonment for a term not exceeding [specified number of months], or both;
    b. on conviction on indictment, to a fine or imprisonment for a term not exceeding [specified number of years], or both.

**Review**
7. These Regulations shall be reviewed at an interval of [specified number of years] from the date of coming into force, to ensure they remain fit for purpose and effectively contribute to public safety.

Signed by authority of the Secretary of State

[Name of the Secretary of State]
Secretary of State for the Home Department

---

This hypothetical statutory instrument outlines a framework for collecting and verifying purchaser information for explosive, caustic, and corrosive materials, with the aim of enhancing public safety and traceability of hazardous materials. This document is for illustrative purposes only and does not reflect actual UK legislation.

In the context of a hypothetical, using the language of a British Statutory instrument, "Waste (Low level Radioactive Sources)  " - Some waste items like,  smoke detectors contain 'trace' radioactive sources, that should not necessarily go in landfill, this regulation would have the intent of requiring Local authority waste collection authorities to make appropriate filtering and removal provisions for such materials.

Here’s a hypothetical British Statutory Instrument regarding the handling of waste containing low-level radioactive sources:

---

**Statutory Instruments**

**2023 No. XXXX**

**Environmental Protection**

**Radioactive Substances**

**The Waste (Low-Level Radioactive Sources) Regulations 2023**

Made - - - - [date]

Laid before Parliament - - - - [date]

Coming into force - - - - [date]

The Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, in exercise of the powers conferred by sections [relevant sections] of the Environmental Protection Act 1990 (c. 43), and the Radioactive Substances Act 1993 (c. 12), and after consultation with the Health and Safety Executive, the Environment Agency, and [other relevant stakeholders], hereby makes the following Regulations:

**Citation, Commencement, and Application**
1. (1) These Regulations may be cited as the Waste (Low-Level Radioactive Sources) Regulations 2023 and come into force on [date].
    (2) They apply in England, Wales, Scotland, and Northern Ireland.

**Interpretation**
2. In these Regulations:
    a. “low-level radioactive sources” refers to materials that contain trace amounts of radioactive substances typically found in household or commercial waste items such as smoke detectors, exit signs, and certain types of medical equipment;
    b. “local authority waste collection authorities” are those entities responsible for the collection and disposal of waste within a specified locality;
    c. “appropriate filtering and removal provisions” refer to the procedures and facilities established to segregate, safely handle, and dispose of waste items containing low-level radioactive sources.

**Duties of Local Authority Waste Collection Authorities**
3. (1) Local authority waste collection authorities must:
    a. implement appropriate filtering and removal provisions for the segregation and collection of waste items containing low-level radioactive sources, to ensure such items are not disposed of in general landfill waste streams;
    b. provide clear guidance to residents and businesses within their jurisdiction on how to dispose of waste items containing low-level radioactive sources;
    c. arrange for the safe and secure storage and transport of segregated waste to designated disposal facilities licensed to receive low-level radioactive waste.

**Training and Compliance**
4. Local authority waste collection authorities must ensure that their staff are appropriately trained in handling and segregating waste items containing low-level radioactive sources in accordance with relevant health and safety legislation.

**Penalties**
5. (1) A local authority waste collection authority that fails to comply with these Regulations commits an offence.
    (2) An individual found to be disposing of low-level radioactive sources in a manner not compliant with the local authority’s guidance also commits an offence.
    (3) A person or entity guilty of an offence under these Regulations shall be liable on summary conviction to a fine not exceeding level [specified level on the standard scale].

**Review**
6. These Regulations shall be reviewed within [specified number of years] of coming into force to ensure they reflect current scientific understanding and waste management best practices.

Signed by authority of the Secretary of State.

[Name]
Secretary of State for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs

---

This hypothetical statutory instrument creates a framework for the management of waste containing low-level radioactive sources, placing obligations on local authority waste collection authorities to segregate and safely handle such waste. It is designed for illustrative purposes and is not a reflection of current UK legislation.

In the context of a hypothetical, using the language of a British Statutory instrument,  Create a voluntary enthusiast photo pass scheme, that sincere railfan photographers can use to deal with 'difficult' staff that doubt their "bona-fides", This does not affect the usual provisions about 'casual' photos though.    (The idea is that the pass 'annual' with a photo, can be used as a de-facto ID to staff.  It still doesn't let them into non-public areas or trackside obviously)

Here's how a British Statutory Instrument might be drafted to establish a voluntary enthusiast photo pass scheme for railfans:

---

**Statutory Instruments**

**2023 No. XXXX**

**Transport**

**Railways**

**The Railways (Enthusiast Photography Pass) Scheme Regulations 2023**

Made - - - - [date]

Laid before Parliament - - - - [date]

Coming into force - - - - [date]

The Secretary of State for Transport, in the interest of promoting railway heritage and the hobby of railway enthusiasm, and after consultation with [relevant stakeholders, including national railway companies and enthusiast communities], hereby makes the following Regulations:

**Citation, Commencement, and Application**
1. (1) These Regulations may be cited as the Railways (Enthusiast Photography Pass) Scheme Regulations 2023.
    (2) They come into force on [date] and apply to England, Wales, Scotland, and Northern Ireland.

**Interpretation**
2. In these Regulations:
    a. “Enthusiast Photography Pass” (the Pass) refers to a voluntary identification pass issued to sincere railway enthusiasts for the purpose of photography on railway premises;
    b. "railfan" refers to an individual with an avid interest in rail transport and photography thereof;
    c. "public areas" refers to those parts of railway premises to which the public is ordinarily granted access without the need for appointment or special admission.

**The Enthusiast Photography Pass Scheme**
3. (1) The Secretary of State shall establish the Enthusiast Photography Pass Scheme, which provides for the issuance of a Pass to railfans upon application.
    (2) The Pass shall serve as recognition of the holder's bona fide interest in railway photography and shall not confer any rights of access beyond those available to the general public.

**Provisions of the Pass**
4. (1) The Pass shall be issued annually and include the following:
    a. a recent photograph of the holder;
    b. the holder's name and membership number;
    c. an expiry date, one year from the date of issue.
    (2) The Pass shall contain no additional personal information to safeguard the holder's privacy.

**Application for the Pass**
5. (1) Applications for the Pass shall be made to the authority designated by the Secretary of State, together with:
    a. a completed application form;
    b. proof of identity acceptable to the designated authority;
    c. a current photograph conforming to the standards set by the designated authority;
    d. the prescribed fee, if applicable.

**Use of the Pass**
6. (1) The holder of the Pass may present it to railway staff as evidence of their sincere intent to engage in non-commercial, enthusiast photography in public areas of railway premises.
    (2) The Pass does not grant the holder:
    a. any right of entry to non-public areas;
    b. any exemption from the general regulations governing railway premises, including those concerning the prohibition of tripods, flash photography, and restriction in circumstances of operational necessity or safety.

**Recognition of the Pass by Rail Operators**
7. All rail operators shall recognize the validity of the Pass and respect its intent, ensuring that staff are trained to acknowledge it and accommodate railfans in their enthusiasm for railway photography, within the bounds of safety and operational constraints.

**Offenses and Penalties**
8. It is an offence to falsify information in the application for the Pass or to use a counterfeit Pass. Any individual found guilty of such an offence is liable on summary conviction to a fine not exceeding level [specified level on the standard scale].

**Review**
9. The Enthusiast Photography Pass Scheme shall be subject to review every [specified number of years] to assess its effectiveness and applicability, with potential amendments made as necessary.

Signed by authority of the Secretary of State.

[Name of the Secretary of State]
Secretary of State for Transport

---

This hypothetical statutory instrument sets out the establishment of a voluntary Enthusiast Photography Pass for sincere railway enthusiasts to assist them when interacting with staff while conducting railway photography in public areas. This draft is illustrative and created for the purpose of this example.

In the context of a hypothetical, using the language of a British Statutory instrument, create a "Bird nesting non-interference Byelaw" for the fictional Town of "Chalfont St Alban" , that prohibits interference with nesting birds such a Ducks or Geese on the park ponds during such season as the bird shall be mating, nesting, rearing etc...  on penalty of a to be determined fine.

Here's a hypothetical British Statutory Instrument for a byelaw in the fictional town of Chalfont St Alban concerning the non-interference with nesting birds:

---

**Statutory Instruments**

**2023 No. XXXX**

**Environment**

**Wildlife Conservation**

**The Chalfont St Alban (Bird Nesting Non-Interference) Byelaws 2023**

Made - - - - [date]

Laid before the Council of Chalfont St Alban - - - - [date]

Coming into force - - - - [date]

The Town Council of Chalfont St Alban, in exercise of the powers conferred upon them by section [relevant section(s)] of the Local Government Act 1972, and of all other powers enabling them in that behalf, and after consultation with [relevant environmental and wildlife conservation bodies], hereby make the following byelaws for the protection of avian wildlife within their jurisdiction:

**Citation and Commencement**
1. (1) These Byelaws may be cited as the Chalfont St Alban (Bird Nesting Non-Interference) Byelaws 2023 and shall come into force on [date].

**Interpretation**
2. In these Byelaws:
    a. “bird” refers specifically to avian species known to nest within the parks and public waterways of Chalfont St Alban, including but not limited to, ducks and geese;
    b. “nesting season” refers to the period during which birds are actively mating, nesting, or rearing their young, as recognized by common environmental practice, extending generally from [specific start date] to [specific end date] each year.

**Non-Interference with Nesting Birds**
3. (1) A person shall not, during the nesting season:
    a. wilfully or negligently disturb or interfere with any bird known to be nesting or rearing its young on the ponds or within the parks of Chalfont St Alban;
    b. damage, remove, or destroy the nests of any such bird while it is in use or being built;
    c. obstruct or prevent any such bird from accessing its nesting site.

**Penalty**
4. (1) Any person who contravenes the provisions of these Byelaws shall be liable on summary conviction to a fine not exceeding level [to be determined] on the standard scale.

**Enforcement**
5. (1) Officers or authorized agents of the Chalfont St Alban Town Council, along with any other persons deemed competent by the Council, shall be responsible for enforcing these Byelaws.
    (2) Members of the public are encouraged to report any violations of these Byelaws to the Town Council or to local law enforcement.

**Exemptions**
6. These Byelaws shall not apply to a person who:
    a. carries out an authorized operation as an officer or agent of a statutory body in the interest of public health, safety, or other urgent reason;
    b. can demonstrate such action was necessary to alleviate suffering or distress of a bird, under veterinary guidance if possible.

**Review**
7. These Byelaws shall be subject to review [number] years subsequent to their commencement.

Made by the Chalfont St Alban Town Council on [date].
[Seal of the Chalfont St Alban Town Council]

Signed by [Name]
[Position in the Council]
(For and on behalf of the Chalfont St Alban Town Council)

Verified by [Name]
[Position in the Council]

Date: [date of verification]

---

This hypothetical statutory instrument is meant to illustrate how a local government might legally protect avian species during critical periods of their life cycle through the implementation of a byelaw. The final document would consider the nuances of wildlife protection laws and be informed by relevant environmental data and public feedback.

In the context of a hypothetical, using the language of a British Local Statutory instrument for the fictional Town of “Chalfont St Alban” , draft a 'Transfer of lands' from the British Railways Board Resdiual lands to the town council,  being the former uncompleted trackbed of a railway from a point immediately North of the current active railway station , to 1km roughly northwest.  The land is to be used for a linear park.

Here is a hypothetical example of a Local Statutory Instrument concerning the transfer of land from the British Railways Board Residual to the Town Council of the fictional town, Chalfont St Alban, for the creation of a linear park:

---

**Statutory Instruments**

**2023 No. XXXX**

**Local Government**

**The Chalfont St Alban (Transfer of Lands from the British Railways Board Residual) Order 2023**

Made - - - - [date]

Coming into force - - - - [date]

The Secretary of State for Transport, in exercise of the powers conferred by the British Railways Act 2005 and sections [relevant sections] of the Localism Act 2011, and after consultation with the British Railways Board Residual and the Town Council of Chalfont St Alban, hereby makes the following Order:

**Citation and Commencement**
1. (1) This Order may be cited as the Chalfont St Alban (Transfer of Lands from the British Railways Board Residual) Order 2023 and shall come into force on [date].

**Interpretation**
2. In this Order:
    a. "the Board" means the British Railways Board Residual;
    b. "the Council" means the Town Council of Chalfont St Alban;
    c. "the land" refers to the former uncompleted trackbed of a railway extending from a point immediately north of the current active railway station in Chalfont St Alban, to a point 1km roughly northwest thereof;
    d. "linear park" refers to a public park that is significantly longer than it is wide, usually following a strip of land alongside a road, river, or disused railway line.

**Transfer of the Land**
3. (1) The land specified in article 2(c) is hereby transferred from the Board to the Council.
    (2) The Council is to hold the land for the purposes of the creation, development, and subsequent maintenance of a linear park for the benefit of the public.
  
**Conditions of the Transfer**
4. The transfer of the land is subject to the following conditions:
    a. the Council shall not use the land for any purpose other than as a linear park without prior written consent from the Secretary of State for Transport;
    b. the Council shall maintain the land in a condition suitable for public use as a park;
    c. the Council shall grant reasonable access to the land to members of the public throughout the year during daylight hours, subject to necessary closures for maintenance or public safety.

**Transfer Arrangements**
5. (1) The transfer shall take effect on a date to be agreed upon by the Board and the Council, which shall not be later than [specified date].
    (2) The terms of the transfer, including any financial arrangements, shall be documented in a transfer agreement to be negotiated and agreed upon by both the Board and the Council.

Signed by authority of the Secretary of State.

[Name of the Minister]
Minister of State,
Department for Transport

---

This hypothetical local statutory instrument provides a framework for the transfer of ownership of a tract of land from the British Railways Board Residual to a local town council for the purpose of creating a public park. It outlines conditions that ensure the land will be used for its intended purpose and will remain accessible to the public. Please note that this is a creative example for illustrative purposes and is not reflective of actual UK law.
